llm.rationale: Freedom to discuss matters without external constraint or humiliation; the speaker laments inability to debate freely, indicating a claim about freedom from interference with parliamentary discussion.

sentence: be dispensed with and that the Duke of Newcastle be at liberty to sign before the Prorogation of Parliament, the Protest entered against the Third Reading of the Bill, although he was not present when the question was put.
speaker: Unknown

llm.label: other
llm.rationale: The Duke of Newcastle is "at liberty" to sign a Protest—this is parliamentary procedure language meaning he is permitted or allowed to perform this procedural act despite his absence.

sentence:
Gentleman spoke about the members of his party not invoking those powers, I take the liberty of reminding him that the very
 974 
last time when an accusation of personal corruption was brought against Members of this House, Sir Stafford Northcote, who was Leader of the House and Chancellor of the Exchequer, brought it forward, and moved two resolutions—first, that it was a breach of the privilege of Parliament, and, second, that the man guilty of it should be brought to the bar of the House.
speaker: MR. JOHN REDMOND

llm.label: other
llm.rationale: The phrase "I take the liberty of reminding him" is parliamentary procedural language where "liberty" means permission or opportunity granted by convention, not a substantive claim about freedom-as-a-value.

sentence: The representative of Switzer land speaking for a country which had fought in vindication of its liberty—
speaker: *SIR WILLIAM HARCOURT  (Monmouthshire, W.)

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: Switzerland fought in vindication of its liberty—a rhetorical invocation of national freedom from external interference or conquest.

sentence: Gentleman went on to denounce a statement which I had made with regard to our perfect freedom to employ natives in any future war, and he talked about the days of Chatham and the employment of Indians in the war with America.
speaker: MR. J. CHAMBERLAIN

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: The speaker asserts freedom (non-interference) to employ natives in future military operations—freedom from restriction on strategic decisions of war.

sentence: If this war was being carried on, not between Great Britain and these two Republics, but between France or Germany, and some petty State, would not the sympathy of every man in this country be with the men who were fighting for their freedom?
speaker: MR. BRYCE

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: Men fighting for their freedom: fighting to resist external military domination by a great power, i.e., freedom from foreign conquest and interference with self-governance.

sentence: And the third is, both as regards the white and the native population, to lay, at any rate, the first stone in the foundation of what we all hope may be an enduring fabric of liberty and justice.
speaker: MR. ASQUITH  (Fifeshire, E,)

llm.label: ambiguous
llm.rationale: The sentence invokes "liberty" as an abstract ideal—an aspirational value being built as part of a social fabric alongside justice—without specifying what people are being freed from or to what capacity they are being empowered.

sentence: Some time ago I went to Gara Hill, near Athenry, in the county of Galway, for the purpose of addressing a public meeting and advocating the principles of the United Irish League, which is a perfectly legal organisation, and the constitution of which is based on the principles of liberty, justice, and equality.
speaker: MR. JOHN O'DONNELL  (Mayo, S.)

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: The sentence invokes "liberty" as a foundational principle of the United Irish League's constitution alongside justice and equality—a rhetorical invocation of liberty as a cause opposing tyranny and oppression, characteristic of negative liberty discourse.

sentence:
In Munster and Connaught a man's liberty is endangered and his county proclaimed if he refuses to speak
 1066 
to some person whom he rightly regards as an enemy to his class and his race.
speaker: MR. JOHN O'DONNELL

llm.label: negative_liberty

sentence: I may take the liberty of telling the Chief Secretary that the sweetness of tongue and emptiness of promise mixed, as it has been, with a great deal of the callousness and brutality of the worst of his predecessors which has so far characterised him, will no longer be tolerated.
speaker: MR. JOHN O'DONNELL

llm.label: other
llm.rationale: The phrase "take the liberty of telling" is a parliamentary procedural expression meaning "permit myself to speak"; it does not make a substantive claim about liberty as a value, and does not name what is being freed or from-or-to what.

sentence: He hoped that the House of Commons would try to regain some of its liberty of criticism and control, which had been rapidly passing away from it.
speaker: *MR. HERBERT LEWIS

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: The sentence concerns the House of Commons' freedom from executive constraint in exercising its critical and supervisory functions; liberty of criticism and control is a negative liberty—freedom from interference with parliamentary oversight.

sentence: In fact, having regard to the way in which the powers already conferred upon them have been abused, it may be a matter for subsequent consideration whether we should not go back to the Constitution of Malta as it existed before 1887, and under which the Maltese enjoyed full liberty and prosperity.
speaker: THE SECRETARY OF STATE FOR THE COLONIES (Mr. J. CHAMBERLAIN, Birmingham, W.)

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: The Maltese "enjoyed full liberty and prosperity" under the 1887 Constitution—liberty here refers to freedom from arbitrary governmental interference and a condition of self-governance and civic well-being, contrasted with the abuse of powers that followed.

sentence: There had been rioting in Ireland, and an Orangemen or two may have been in it, but recently the Grand Orange Lodge of Belfast had passed a resolution against these outrages, and against interference with the liberty of the subject in Belfast.
speaker: *MR. WILLIAM JOHNSTON  (Belfast, S.)

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: The subject (citizens in Belfast) is being freed from interference/outrage. This is a claim about protection from external impediment to one's personal liberty.

sentence: In 1872 he, being in Canada, was thanked by a body of Irishmen for having helped to obtain this extension of freedom, and Mr.
speaker: *MR. WILLIAM JOHNSTON  (Belfast, S.)

llm.label: positive_liberty
llm.rationale: An extension of freedom (most likely electoral or political rights, such as franchise extension for Irish voters) was obtained; the person helped secure this expansion of citizens' capacity to participate in governance.

sentence: On the contrary, we are securing to them the liberty to make their own choice, and it is only because that choice seems to have been against the feelings of the elected members, who are chiefly lawyers, that this agitation has arisen.
speaker: THE SECRETARY OF STATE FOR THE COLONIES (Mr. J. CHAMBERLAIN, Birmingham, W.)

llm.label: positive_liberty
llm.rationale: The object of freedom is "to make their own choice" — an empowered capacity being secured to a group, not merely non-interference with choice-making. This is freedom TO exercise agency.
